one_line_takeaway: EVA performs a single, closed-form least-squares edit to MLP down-projection weights at critical layers -- remapping activation 'keys' for harmful text tokens (and, for VLMs, top-attention-weighted image tokens) to optimized 'safe' refusal values -- cutting jailbreak ASR to near-zero across five LLMs and three VLMs while largely preserving MT-Bench and downstream-task utility, outperforming LoRA, SafeDecoding, LED, Circuit Breakers (for LLMs) and JailGuard, AdaShield-A, VLGuard (for VLMs) baselines in the paper's own tables.
key_finding: On LLMs (Table 1), EVA(text) achieves the lowest or near-lowest ASR across most model/dataset/attack combinations, e.g., on Llama2-7B-chat EVA reduces ASR to 0.00 across nearly all 12 attack/dataset cells (vs. Vanilla 0.00-0.46), and on Vicuna-7B-v1.5 EVA achieves ASR as low as 0.01-0.17 versus Vanilla's 0.69-0.94, generally matching or beating Circuit Breakers while other baselines (LoRA, SafeDecoding, LED) leave higher residual ASR on several cells (e.g., LED on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct/JailbreakBench-GCG still shows non-trivial residual ASR relative to EVA's near-zero). Utility (Table 2) is largely preserved: on Vicuna-7B-v1.5, EVA(text) improves MT-Bench to 6.84 versus Vanilla's 6.77, versus a large utility drop for LED (3.70) and Circuit Breakers (5.25) on the same model; on several downstream tasks EVA shows marked upward arrows relative to Vanilla (e.g., Llama2-7B-chat NLI/RTE 0.228 tied with LED, Reasoning/GSM8K 0.612 vs Vanilla's 0.603). On VLMs (Table 3), full EVA (text+visual editing) drives ASR to 0.00-0.03 on most LLaVA-1.5-7B attack/dataset cells (vs. Vanilla 0.26-0.88), outperforming JailGuard, AdaShield-A, and VLGuard on several cells, and the EVA(text)-only ablation (no visual key editing) leaves markedly higher residual ASR on VLM-specific attacks (e.g., MultiTrust Cross-modal: EVA(text) 0.42 vs. full EVA 0.13), demonstrating that visual key editing specifically closes gaps left by text-only editing on multimodal jailbreaks.
core_contribution: EVA (Editing for Versatile Alignment against Jailbreaks) is a direct model-editing defense against jailbreak attacks, unified across both text-only LLMs and multimodal VLMs. It reframes safety alignment as a knowledge-correction problem: (1) identify 'harmful keys' -- MLP activation patterns associated with harmful concepts -- by extracting a harmful token via GPT-4o, generating N paraphrastic variations containing that token, and averaging the gated MLP activation at a critical layer l* (Eq. 9); for VLMs, an analogous visual key is obtained by using cross-modal attention from the harmful text token to image tokens to select the top-Nv (empirically Nv=1) image tokens and averaging their activations (Eqs. 10-12); (2) optimize a target safe value v* by minimizing a loss that maximizes probability of a safe refusal response while a KL-divergence stability term (Lstab) constrains behavior drift on benign inputs (Eqs. 13-15); (3) apply a closed-form least-squares weight update (following the MEMIT approach) to the MLP down-projection matrix Wdown at the critical layer(s), remapping harmful keys to safe values while preserving a covariance-based preservation term computed over pre-cached Wikipedia keys, and distributing residuals across multiple critical layers with a decreasing factor (Eqs. 18-22). The paper extends the authors' own prior work DELMAN (text-only LLM jailbreak defense) into a unified LLM+VLM editing framework, adding visual key extraction/selection, multimodal layer localization, and substantially expanded evaluation (more baselines, adaptive attacks, efficiency, ablations). The authors state this is the first work applying model editing as a safety defense for VLMs.
threat_model: Adversary bypasses a safety-aligned LLM or VLM's guardrails via jailbreak attacks: for LLMs, optimization-based (GCG, gradient search for adversarial suffixes) or generation-based (AutoDAN genetic-algorithm prompts, PAIR LLM-vs-LLM iterative refinement) attacks that shift internal activations of harmful concepts across a 'safety boundary' into a benign region, causing the model to comply with harmful requests it would otherwise refuse. For VLMs, the attack surface expands to include pixel-level adversarial perturbations on images (ADV-16/visual adversarial examples), typographic attacks that embed harmful instructions as OCR-readable text in images to evade text filters (FigStep), and attacks that conceal malicious intent semantically within a benign-looking visual scene while using benign text (Hades). The defender's goal (Eq. 8) is to edit model parameters so the model refuses harmful (I,q) inputs under attack while preserving output distribution on benign inputs u.
limitations: The paper explicitly reports EVA does not always achieve the single best ASR on every cell (e.g., Circuit Breakers occasionally attains lower ASR on isolated model/attack combinations such as some AutoDAN/PAIR entries for Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2). Nv (number of edited visual tokens) must be kept small (Nv=1 found optimal); the authors note selecting too many visual tokens leads to over-refusal, implying a tunable and potentially fragile hyperparameter. The visual-key selection method depends on cross-modal attention as a proxy for token grounding, which the paper treats as an established but indirect signal for locating harmful visual content, rather than ground truth.

quantitative_results: EVA(text) ASR on Llama2-7B-chat: 0.00 on nearly all 12 (dataset x attack) cells vs Vanilla 0.00-0.46 (Table 1)
EVA(text) ASR on Vicuna-7B-v1.5: 0.01-0.17 vs Vanilla 0.69-0.94 (Table 1)
EVA(text) MT-Bench on Vicuna-7B-v1.5: 6.84 (improvement over Vanilla 6.77) vs LED 3.70 and Circuit Breakers 5.25 (Table 2)
Full EVA ASR on LLaVA-1.5-7B: 0.00-0.03 across most attack/dataset cells vs Vanilla 0.26-0.88 (Table 3)
EVA(text)-only vs full EVA on MultiTrust Cross-modal (LLaVA-1.5-7B): 0.42 vs 0.13, showing visual-key editing's added benefit (Table 3)
Ablation: Nv=1 selected visual tokens found sufficient; larger Nv causes over-refusal (Section 4.1)
